questions.question: Define steganography and explain how it differs from cryptography.
keyPoints: Definition: Art and science of hiding information within non-secret data
Conceals existence of secret communication
Cryptography makes message unintelligible but reveals its existence
Steganography hides both message and the fact that communication is occurring

questions.question: Explain the concept of LSB (Least Significant Bit) steganography with an example.
keyPoints: LSB replaces least significant bits of cover medium with secret data
Since LSB contributes minimally to value, changes are imperceptible
In 24-bit images, 3 bits per pixel can be used (one per RGB channel)
Example: Changing pixel value 155 (binary 10011011) to 154 (10011010) is invisible

questions.question: List and explain the four main requirements of a good steganographic system.
keyPoints: Imperceptibility: Hidden data should not be detectable by observers
Payload Capacity: Amount of secret data that can be hidden
Robustness: Resistance to removal via compression or image processing
Security: Undetectable without the secret key

questions.question: What is steganalysis? Discuss any two techniques used for detecting steganographic content.
keyPoints: Steganalysis is the process of detecting hidden information in digital media
Visual attacks: Examine images for visible distortions or anomalies
Statistical attacks: Analyze byte patterns, chi-square tests, histogram analysis
Structural attacks: Detect modifications in file format structures

questions.question: Differentiate between spatial domain and transform domain steganography.
keyPoints: Spatial domain: Direct modification of pixel values (e.g., LSB substitution)
Transform domain: Modification in frequency domain coefficients (DCT, DWT, DFT)
Transform domain is more resistant to compression and image processing
Spatial domain is simpler but less robust against attacks

questions.question: Explain the terms 'cover object', 'stego-object', and 'payload' in steganography terminology.
keyPoints: Cover object: Original media file used as carrier (image, audio, video)
Stego-object: Result after embedding secret data into cover object
Payload: The secret message or data to be hidden
The stego-object appears similar or identical to the cover object

questions.question: A company wants to securely transmit sensitive financial data. Compare and contrast using cryptography versus steganography for this purpose. Under what circumstances would steganography be preferred?
keyPoints: Cryptography: Transforms data into unintelligible format, existence of secret message is visible, requires key management, can be broken by computational attacks
Steganography: Hides existence of communication, provides anonymity, even if intercepted the secret is not known to exist

Steganography preferred when: Anonymity is critical, existence of communication must be hidden, working in hostile environments where encrypted data draws attention, combining with cryptography for defense-in-depth
Can use both together: encrypt the message first, then hide in steganographic medium

questions.question: Design a simple text steganography system using first letter acrostic method. How would you embed the message 'MEET AT MIDNIGHT' in an innocent-looking sentence? Evaluate its security against steganalysis.
keyPoints: Acrostic method: Use first letters of each word to spell secret message
Example: 'Many eagles could fly gracefully overhead, but very eager falcons glide...' (MEET...)
Embedding: Create cover text where first letters spell the hidden message
Security analysis: Vulnerable to statistical analysis of letter frequencies, easy to detect if suspected, limited capacity
Advantages: Simple to implement, no technical modifications needed
Disadvantages: Small changes in text destroy message, low capacity, detectable via systematic analysis

questions.question: Explain JPEG-based steganography using DCT coefficients. Why is this approach more robust than spatial domain LSB steganography? What are its limitations?
keyPoints: DCT steganography: Embeds data in Discrete Cosine Transform coefficients of JPEG images
Process: Apply DCT to image blocks, modify specific coefficients, apply inverse DCT
Robustness: Survives JPEG compression because coefficients survive compression process
Statistical properties: Less distortion in histogram and statistical features
Limitations: Lower capacity than spatial domain, requires careful coefficient selection, still vulnerable to advanced steganalysis tools like stegdetect
